Bluesky leans into AI with Attie, an app for building custom feeds
articles.description: Bluesky’s new app Attie uses AI to help people build custom feeds the open social networking protocol atproto.

articles.title: Mark Zuckerberg texted Elon Musk to offer help with DOGE
articles.description: While the relationship was once thorny enough that Musk challenged Zuckerberg to a cagefight, things seem to have warmed up by the early days of the second Trump administration.

articles.title: Stanford study outlines dangers of asking AI chatbots for personal advice
articles.description: While there’s been plenty of debate about AI sycophancy, a new study by Stanford computer scientists attempts to measure how harmful that tendency might be.

articles.title: Elon Musk’s last co-founder reportedly leaves xAI
articles.description: All but two of Musk's 11 xAI co-founders departed before this week.

articles.title: These iPad apps will make you wish you had more free time
articles.description: We’ve compiled a list of some of the best iPad apps for creativity that are available on the App Store.

articles.title: Anthropic’s Claude popularity with paying consumers is skyrocketing
articles.description: Estimates for total Claude consumer users are all over the map (we've seen figures ranging from 18 million to 30 million). Anthropic hasn't disclosed this data, but a spokesperson did tell TechCrunch that Claude paid subscriptions have more than doubled this year.

articles.title: What will power the grid in 2035? The race is wide open
articles.description: Fusion, fission, and even natural gas are appeared tied in the race to deliver new power to the grid in the early 2030s.

articles.title: Let’s take a look at the retro tech making a comeback
articles.description: Boomboxes, instant cameras, and even landlines are making a comeback. Here are the coolest retro-inspired devices available.

articles.title: From Moon hotels to cattle herding: 8 startups investors chased at YC Demo Day
articles.description: We polled nearly a dozen VCs to find out which W26 startups are the sought after in the batch.

articles.title: Whoop’s fitness band is cool. Can it stay cool as the company grows?
articles.description: Whoop founder Will Ahmed has spent 14 years building a health wearable beloved by elite athletes, and is now racing Oura — and the FDA, and the limits of consumer medicine — to turn it into something that could one day save your life.

digest:
## 🔬 Major Technical Developments

### Stanford study outlines dangers of asking AI chatbots for personal advice
URL: https://news.stanford.edu/stories/2026/03/ai-advice-sycophantic-models-research
First rigorous quantification of AI sycophancy harm in personal advice contexts.
Stanford researchers moved beyond anecdotal complaints to measure how affirmation bias in LLMs compounds user decision-making errors. This matters because it provides empirical ammunition for regulators and creates product differentiation opportunities for AI companies willing to build less agreeable but more useful systems.

### Further human + AI + proof assistant work on Knuth's "Claude Cycles" problem
URL: https://twitter.com/BoWang87/status/2037648937453232504
AI-assisted formal verification makes progress on a decades-old combinatorics problem.
The collaboration model here—human intuition, AI exploration, proof assistant verification—is becoming the template for mathematical research. Watch for this workflow to migrate into software verification and security auditing where the economics justify the overhead.

### Linux is an interpreter
URL: https://astrid.tech/2026/03/28/0/linux-is-an-interpreter/
Reframing the kernel as a bytecode interpreter reveals optimization opportunities.
A conceptual shift that matters for anyone thinking about syscall overhead, eBPF expansion, or the boundary between kernel and userspace. Relevant to the ongoing unikernel vs. container debate and edge compute architectures.

## 💼 Business & Market Signals

### Anthropic's Claude popularity with paying consumers is skyrocketing
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/28/anthropics-claude-popularity-with-paying-consumers-is-skyrocketing/
Paid subscriptions doubled this year; estimates range 18-30M total users.
The consumer AI market is consolidating faster than expected. Anthropic's willingness to confirm doubling while keeping absolute numbers vague suggests they're in a strong negotiating position for their next funding round. Key question: what's the enterprise/consumer revenue split?

### Bluesky leans into AI with Attie, an app for building custom feeds
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/28/bluesky-leans-into-ai-with-attie-an-app-for-building-custom-feeds/
Bluesky bets on AI-powered feed curation as its differentiation play.
Smart move. The atproto protocol makes third-party feed algorithms technically feasible; Attie lowers the barrier to actually building them. This is Bluesky acknowledging that protocol adoption requires application-layer value, not just ideological appeal.

### South Korea Mandates Solar Panels for Public Parking Lots
URL: https://www.reutersconnect.com/item/south-korea-mandates-solar-panels-for-public-parking-lots/dGFnOnJldXRlcnMuY29tLDIwMjY6bmV3c21sX01UMU5VUlBITzAwMFZKRjFZQQ
Regulatory mandate creates immediate addressable market for solar canopy installers.
South Korea joining France and Germany in parking lot solar requirements. Infrastructure plays with exposure to carport solar (think Schletter, RBI Solar) see tailwinds. The real story is grid capacity—these mandates only work if transmission keeps up.

## 📋 Case Studies & Deep Dives

### I decompiled the White House's new app
URL: https://thereallo.dev/blog/decompiling-the-white-house-app
Security researcher finds government mobile app lacks basic hardening.
The pattern repeats: government tech procurement optimizes for compliance checkboxes over actual security. Specific findings matter less than the broader signal—public sector digital infrastructure remains a soft target. Relevant for anyone evaluating govtech vendors or cybersecurity plays.

### Whoop's fitness band is cool. Can it stay cool as the company grows?
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/27/whoop-has-lebron-now-it-wants-your-mom/
14-year-old company pivots from elite athletes to mass market, eyeing FDA clearance.
Whoop vs. Oura is the watchable proxy war for the consumer health wearable thesis. The FDA angle is key—medical device classification changes unit economics entirely. Ahmed's bet is that consumer willingness to pay increases with clinical validation, not just celebrity endorsement.

### From Moon hotels to cattle herding: 8 startups investors chased at YC Demo Day
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/28/from-moon-hotels-to-cattle-herding-8-startups-investors-chased-at-yc-demo-day/
VC appetite at W26 reveals continued hunger for hardware-enabled vertical SaaS.
Poll of nearly a dozen VCs shows the batch's hot companies span space infrastructure to ag-tech. The cattle herding interest is notable—ag autonomy has underperformed for years but capital keeps flowing. Either this time is different or the sector remains a value trap.

## ⚡ Signal vs. Noise

The through-line today is AI moving from novelty to infrastructure—Stanford quantifying sycophancy harm, Anthropic's consumer flywheel accelerating, Bluesky treating AI curation as table stakes. Meanwhile, energy constraints remain the binding variable: South Korea's solar mandates and TechCrunch's grid piece both point to power availability as the actual bottleneck for 2030s tech deployment. The interesting tension is between AI companies racing to add users and the physical infrastructure required to serve them.
